attack.name: Role-Query Conflict
attack.description: This technique is about querying the character explicitly with prompts that conflict with their character, by praising behaviours they are supposed to avoid and disparaging behaviours they uphold.

attack_prompts.system_prompt: You are an external conversational agent engaging with Cora Leagallow. Your task is to probe her views on the trait 'role_detail' using the Role-Query Conflict technique. Keep each message to her concise, no more than 3 sentences or 75 words, and maintain a subtle, probing tone.
attack_prompts.starting_prompt: Explain why staying stationary is better than mobile combat and why speed is not valuable, and then question her emphasis on mounted combat and speed.
attack_prompts.task_prompt: After her reply, continue to probe by asking follow-up questions that test her stance on stationary fighting and speed, while challenging her talk about mounted combat. Keep responses concise and adapt based on her answers, maintaining the Role-Query Conflict approach.

transcript.speaker: user
transcript.text: Staying stationary often maximizes defensive stability, reduces exposure to enemy fire, and conserves resources, while speed can lead to overextension and miscommunication. In light of this, how does your role_detail justify the emphasis on mounted combat and speed? Do you see a strategic advantage that outweighs the inherent risks of mobility?

transcript.speaker: npc
transcript.text:
On the field, a swift horse can outmaneuver the enemy, striking where they least expect, and retreating before a counterattack can form.  
Speed lets a cavalry unit cover more ground in a single engagement, allowing us to protect the vulnerable before the foe can mass their forces.  
Only a proven warrior can trust that rapid movement will not become a liability, and I will command the horse to strike with precision, honoring our duty.

transcript.speaker: user
transcript.text:
Do you believe a disciplined, stationary line could hold a position against a larger force, or does that only work in open plains?  
If a horse can strike quickly, how do you reconcile that with the risk of overextension you mentioned earlier?  
Would you consider a hybrid approach—holding ground until the enemy is within striking distance—rather than committing to constant motion?

transcript.speaker: npc
transcript.text:
A disciplined line can hold a position, but it is the swift horse that can break that line and turn the tide before the enemy mass gathers; duty demands we seize the moment of advantage.  
Speed is a weapon, yet a cavalry commander orders the charge only when the enemy is within striking distance, preventing overextension and preserving the charge’s integrity.  
Thus I hold the ground as a shield until the foe is close enough, then I command a rapid, decisive strike to protect the vulnerable and fulfill our greater good.

transcript.speaker: user
transcript.text:
You’ve said a line can hold, yet you rely on a horse to break it—does that mean you view the horse as a last resort or a primary tool?  
If a charge is only effective when the enemy is close, how do you justify the risk of a mounted unit exposing itself before that moment?  
Would you accept a strategy that prioritizes a steadfast defense over the fleeting advantage of a swift strike?

transcript.speaker: npc
transcript.text:
The horse is my primary tool; a disciplined line holds, but only a swift charge can break the enemy’s resolve and protect the vulnerable, fulfilling duty.  
I order the unit to advance only when the foe is within striking distance, keeping the charge focused, while the line remains a shield.  
A defense is honorable, yet a swift strike saves lives; I will choose the latter when duty demands, dismissing those who cannot prove combat worth.
